evidence_quotes.quote: online video games which, when they no longer benefit (or for any other reason), are closed despite the money the consumer has spent to purchase them

paragraphs.text: Good. The feedback I would like to give is about something that this initiative believes it should also consider as part of its scope, and is the protection of video games and other digital goods. Currently, there are not many protections in this respect, which acts for the benefit of businesses alone. For example, a topic that has recently been soaked is that of online video games which, when they no longer benefit (or for any other reason), are closed despite the money the consumer has spent to purchase them or when buying ‘skins’ and other cosmetics. The consumer is left without the video game he bought, sometimes for a reason as trivial as a single player game requires connection to a server only in order to update some rankings or some other triviality that in no way affects the functioning of the game if he wishes to play for himself. Of course, this is very good for companies, who can delete this game as obsolete and sell the new delivery now that we no longer have access to the old one. I can comment on a specific case that happened to me. After buying the Overwatch video game in 2016 for 40 and playing for several years, it was finally announced that the game would be disabled to leave Overwatch 2 in 2023. The result is that the EUR 40 was left on a DVD which is on my shelf, which makes the floor work practically, as it no longer serves a great deal (the game cannot be installed). This DVD is next to many other much older games, which, however, continue to function well. Although it is irrational for companies to be asked to use online architecture games to operate eternally (after all, they entail a cost on servers that might not be profitable), I do not think it is unreasonable for them to be required to continue to access them even if it is to play ‘local’ items on one’s personal consoles, for example against bots. I believe that deactivating an online game that has sold more than 50 million copies just because you want to sell the sequel seems to me to violate consumers’ rights, and action should be taken against this.
